repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem: Plugins may want to have a way to show more details about items
  when using `vim.ui.select`. This is a fairly common problem that
  prompts plugin authors to implement dedicated sources/pickers for
  fuzzy picker plugins that are popular at the moment.

Solution: Document a way for `vim.ui.select` to provide preview:
  - `vim.ui.select` users can provide `opts.preview_item` function that creates/uses a buffer and its contents at certain position to show more details about an item.
  - `vim.ui.select` implementations may use `opts.preview_item` in the way they see fit (like show the buffer in a separate/same window interactively/on-demand or do nothing) if they have a way to show more information about an item.

---

Resolve #36558

---

Notes:
- Default implementation is not really equipped to provide a preview. I don't think it should be specially documented, since there is a language indicating that `vim.ui.select` implementations may choose to not provide item preview.
- I initially thought `opts.preview_item` returning buffer number should be enough, but:
    - This is not really forward compatible.
    - The case of reusing existing buffer and just pointing at specific position seems fairly common. ~~Maybe also having `pos_end` is reasonable for previewing a region (like for diagnostics, tree-sitter nodes, etc.), but wasn't sure how complicated this is welcome to be.~~ After thinking about it, the only good reason for `pos_end` is to highlight the range, but that is better done for `vim.ui.select` user when creating a buffer.

    So returning a table with information ~~where a buffer id is the only required field~~ should be enough. Missing buffer id is reasonable to interpret like "don't show a preview of this item".

- I am not sure if this needs a 'news.txt' entry.
---

Here is a (fairly naive) example implementation of `vim.ui.select` that has interactive preview:

<details><summary>`vim.ui.select` implementation with interactive preview</summary>

```lua
-- Selection UI with interactive preview.
-- Navigate items as in a regular buffer. If `vim.ui.select()` call provides
-- `opts.preview_item`, there is a second window that shows preview and is
-- updated on every cursor move.
-- Choose with <CR>, close with <Esc>
vim.ui.select = function(items, opts, on_choice)
  local main_win, preview_win
  local width = math.ceil(0.25 * vim.o.columns)
  local height = math.min(vim.o.lines, #items)

  -- Main window
  local main_buf = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
  local lines = vim.tbl_map(opts.format_item or tostring, items)
  vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(main_buf, 0, -1, false, lines)
  vim.bo[main_buf].modifiable = false

  local main_win_opts = { relative = 'editor', row = 0, col = 0, height = height, width = width, border = 'single' }
  main_win = vim.api.nvim_open_win(main_buf, true, main_win_opts)
  vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(main_win, { 1, 0 })
  vim.wo[main_win].wrap = false

  -- Preview window
  local has_preview = vim.is_callable(opts.preview_item)
  local update_preview = function(idx)
    local preview_data = opts.preview_item(items[idx])
    if preview_win == nil or not vim.api.nvim_win_is_valid(preview_win) then
      local preview_win_opts =
        { relative = 'editor', row = 0, col = width + 2, height = height, width = width, border = 'single' }
      preview_win = vim.api.nvim_open_win(preview_data.buf, false, preview_win_opts)
    else
      vim.api.nvim_win_set_buf(preview_win, preview_data.buf)
    end

    vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(preview_win, preview_data.pos or { 1, 0 })
  end
  if not has_preview then update_preview = function(_) end end

  update_preview(1)

  -- Interactive preview
  local preview_at_cursor = function() update_preview(vim.fn.line('.')) end
  if not has_preview then preview_at_cursor = function() end end

  vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd('CursorMoved', { buffer = main_buf, callback = preview_at_cursor })

  -- Choose and close
  local close = function(idx)
    vim.api.nvim_win_close(main_win, true)
    vim.api.nvim_buf_delete(main_buf, { force = true })
    if has_preview then vim.api.nvim_win_close(preview_win, true) end
    on_choice(items[idx], idx)
  end

  local choose = function() close(vim.api.nvim_win_get_cursor(main_win)[1]) end
  vim.keymap.set('n', '<CR>', choose, { buffer = main_buf })

  local quit = function() close() end
  vim.keymap.set('n', '<Esc>', quit, { buffer = main_buf })
end
```

</details>

Here is how to use `vim.ui.select` with preview:

```lua
local preview_number = function(item)
  local buf = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
  vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, 0, -1, false, { 'This is ' .. vim.inspect(item) })
  vim.bo[buf].bufhidden = 'wipe'
  return { buf = buf }
end

vim.ui.select({ 'one', 'two', 'three', 'four', 'five' }, { preview_item = preview_number }, print)
```

Here is a demo of these (`select_number()` is a global function for the above `vim.ui.select` usage):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c32d339b-34ec-4008-a2f0-fac174b0aa4f

Here is how a minimal buffer picker can look like:

```lua
vim.ui.select(vim.api.nvim_list_bufs(), {
  preview_item = function(buf) return { buf = buf } end,
  format_item = function(buf) return vim.api.nvim_buf_get_name(buf) end,
}, function(buf) vim.cmd.buffer(buf) end)
```

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
--- If missing, no special preview is used.
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -10,6 +10,16 @@ local M = {}
 --- individual item from `items`. Defaults to `tostring`.
 ---@field format_item? fun(item: any):string
 ---
+--- Function to preview an individual item from `items`.
+--- If missing, no special preview is required.

@@ -10,6 +10,16 @@ local M = {}
 --- individual item from `items`. Defaults to `tostring`.
 ---@field format_item? fun(item: any):string
 ---
+--- Function to preview an individual item from `items`.
+--- If missing, no special preview is required.
+--- Should ensure a buffer with contents (text, highlighting) providing details about an item.
+--- Should return a table with information that `vim.ui.select` implementations may
+--- use to show the preview buffer in the way they see fit:
+---     - {buf} (`interger`) - buffer id with preview

comment_data.body:
My intention was to convey that if `opts.preview_item` is missing, then `vim.ui.select` implementation is free to either show some default preview or no preview at all. It might be covered by "no *special* preview" part, but not sure.

I guess "used" and "required" is more or less interchangeable here, though.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -10,6 +10,16 @@ local M = {}
 --- individual item from `items`. Defaults to `tostring`.
 ---@field format_item? fun(item: any):string
 ---
+--- Function to preview an individual item from `items`.
+--- If missing, no special preview is required.

comment_regular_data.body: Is there any way to have a barebones default handler for this? That would add weight to this.

> Is there any way to have a barebones default handler for this? That would add weight to this.

I agree and there might be, but my best idea of an implementation would be at least several times larger than the full default `vim.ui.select()` code *and* it won't be particularly intuitive one. All of this comes from the fact that the default `vim.ui.select` [uses `vim.fn.inputlist()`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/9ab6c607ccc73f66ab90721f956c98bbca5cf267/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua#L57) which doesn't have a well defined concept of "current/selected item".

My best suggestion while preserving this current `fn.inputlist()` approach would be:

- Add a temporary `vim.on_key()` before `vim.fn.inputlist()` to react on every key press. This way the "current item" is the one which would have been selected if the user presses `<CR>`. However ...
- ... as far as I can tell, there is no easy/exported way of getting current user input as a string. So it might require tracking key presses and reconstructing the query from them. Which is a lot. But even then ...
- ... selecting from many items may cover the majority of the screen lines. I.e. there is no guarantee for enough space for a preview. Which would presumably be done as a floating window on top of the input selection.

---

Here is the diff which needs more work to be robust and only works with enabled ui2 enabled (to force redraw):

<details><summary>Diff of `vim.ui.select` that adds preview</summary>

```diff
diff --git a/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua b/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
index 8b6aad226d..a8dd9a9949 100644
--- a/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
+++ b/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
@@ -54,7 +54,69 @@ function M.select(items, opts, on_choice)
   do
     table.insert(choices, string.format('%d: %s', i, format_item(item)))
   end
+
+  local pr_win_id
+  local show_preview = function(buf, pos)
+    if type(pr_win_id) == 'number' and vim.api.nvim_win_is_valid(pr_win_id) then
+      vim.api.nvim_win_set_buf(pr_win_id, buf)
+    else
+      -- NOTE: Requires much more robust computation of the config. At least to
+      -- account for complex 'winborder' and not enough space if many items.
+      local border_offset = vim.o.winborder == '' and 0 or 2
+      local config = {
+        relative = 'editor',
+        anchor = 'SW',
+        -- Offset to account for horizontal separator and bottom prompt
+        row = vim.o.lines - #items - border_offset - 1,
+        col = 0,
+        width = vim.o.columns,
+        height = 10,
+        style = 'minimal',
+      }
+      pr_win_id = vim.api.nvim_open_win(buf, false, config)
+    end
+    if pos ~= nil then
+      pcall(vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor, pr_win_id, pos)
+    end
+  end
+
+  local function close_preview()
+    pcall(vim.api.nvim_win_close, pr_win_id, { force = true })
+    pr_win_id = nil
+  end
+
+  local preview_item = opts.preview_item
+  local query = ''
+
+  local function track_query(_, typed)
+    if not vim.is_callable(opts.preview_item) then
+      return
+    end
+
+    -- NOTE: Does not react to deleting text!!!
+    query = query .. typed
+
+    local id = tonumber(query:match('^%d+$'))
+    local item = items[id]
+    if item == nil then
+      return
+    end
+    local pr = preview_item(item)
+    if type(pr) ~= 'table' or type(pr.buf) ~= 'number' or not vim.api.nvim_buf_is_valid(pr.buf) then
+      close_preview()
+    else
+      show_preview(pr.buf)
+    end
+    -- vim.cmd('redraw!')
+    vim.api.nvim__redraw({ flush = true })
+  end
+
+  local ui_ns_id = vim.api.nvim_create_namespace('ui-select-keys')
+  vim.on_key(track_query, ui_ns_id)
   local choice = vim.fn.inputlist(choices)
+  vim.on_key(nil, ui_ns_id)
+  close_preview()
+
   if choice < 1 or choice > #items then
     on_choice(nil, nil)
   else
```

</details>

And here is how it looks with the following 'init.lua':

```lua
local preview_number = function(item)
  local buf = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
  vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, 0, -1, false, { 'This is ' .. vim.inspect(item) })
  vim.bo[buf].bufhidden = 'wipe'
  return { buf = buf }
end

require('vim._core.ui2').enable({ enable = true })

_G.select_number = function()
  vim.ui.select({
    'one',
    'two',
    'three',
    'four',
    'five',
    'six',
    'seven',
    'eight',
    'nine',
    'ten',
  }, { preview_item = preview_number }, print)
end
```

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cc2a40df-2efa-482f-9082-b62ed1922fa7

---

At this point I'd argue that completely reimplementing `vim.ui.select` is less complex, however not urgent. Documenting the interface for `vim.ui.select` implementations is something that is wanted for a long time.
Rebased on `master` and added clarification about missing `buf` and `pos` values in the `preview_item` output.

Thoughts on having the preview function return a list of chunks (text+hl groups), similar to `virt_lines` of the extmark APIs, instead of having it control the buffer?

Currently `vim.ui.select` has a pretty clear separation of concern: The implementation is in full control of the layout, buffer, etc. and the callee/user can only provide the data + a function to render a single item. This ensures that across many plugin's, users still get a somewhat consistent UI.

With this addition this separation is somehow broken and the callers of the `vim.ui.select` function have way more influence on the layout.


And I also think that the default implementation in nvim must support this if we document it.

> Thoughts on having the preview function return a list of chunks (text+hl groups), similar to `virt_lines` of the extmark APIs, instead of having it control the buffer?

I tried to have a cleaner separation, but it all ends up being either too restrictive or way too complicated to implement on the `vim.ui.select()` caller side.

The "list of chunks" approach has at least the following problems:

- It is really, *really* hard to have any meaningful highlighting on the user side beyond really basic "highlight whole line like this". For example, showing a preview of an existing buffer is practically impossible, as there are many things that can add highlighting to a buffer. Similar thing for files and positions in files.
- It does not allow async highlighting, as the chunks need to be pre-computed before returning.

> The implementation is in full control of the layout, buffer, etc. and the callee/user can only provide the data + a function to render a single item. This ensures that across many plugin's, users still get a somewhat consistent UI.
> 
> With this addition this separation is somehow broken and the callers of the `vim.ui.select` function have way more influence on the layout.

That is not the case. Layout is still a responsibility of `vim.ui.select()` implementation. The callers still only provide a data about what to show in the preview, just in the form of the buffer they create. If implementation is not able to handle directly showing the buffer, it is then *its* responsibility to extract highlighting information from the buffer. Or not show preview at all. I.e. make *the use* of `vim.ui.select()` as easier as possible, not the implementation.

> And I also think that the default implementation in nvim must support this if we document it.

I agree that it would be better, but I do not agree with the "must". Documenting interface for something that is meant to be overridable is completely fine. And what matters is that it solves the actual problem in the plugin ecosystem without blocking with the "lets first implement full picker in core before allowing plugins to use other already existing implementations".

> The "list of chunks" approach has at least the following problems:

How about being able to also return a filetype? Would make it similar to how lsp hover works in some way.

> That is not the case. Layout is still a responsibility of vim.ui.select() implementation. The callers still only provide a data about what to show in the preview, just in the form of the buffer they create

Hm fair, although not entirely clear from the current documentation. I think it could use some more additions to clarify the buffer ownership / lifecycle, and that showing the buffer in a window is up to the select implementation.

> Documenting interface for something that is meant to be overridable is completely fine

There's currently not a single word that would tell a user who reads the docs that the preview only works if they also install a plugin that overrides the function with one that has that capability.
Reading a function description and then trying it only to find that it doesn't work as described is an awful experience. 

Which actually raises two more problems/questions:

- Even if core implements it, users could have a plugin that doesn't. We'd need something like https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/37549#issuecomment-3811668488 to ensure users always get the functionality
- How will configuration of this work? I suspect some users might want preview for use-case a), but no preview for use-case b)? Do we tell plugin authors that they should have config options for it. Or should core have some configuration based on `kind`? First instinct is to delegate that to plugins, but we'll also need to think of the `ui.select` uses within core itself.

---

Imho this shouldn't block 0.12 but have some more time to bake.

> > The "list of chunks" approach has at least the following problems:
> 
> How about being able to also return a filetype? Would make it similar to how lsp hover works in some way.

Along with "list of chunks"? Then how would callers indicated that highlighting is needed or not needed.

Creating temporary scratch buffers is cheap and by design (as buffer is an abstraction designed to be shown) covers all possible use cases callers might have to customize preview: highlight or not, set filetype or not, etc.

> Hm fair, although not entirely clear from the current documentation. I think it could use some more additions to clarify the buffer ownership / lifecycle, and that showing the buffer in a window is up to the select implementation.

I think it should be covered by "Should return a table with information that `vim.ui.select` implementations may use to show the preview buffer in the way they see fit". I.e. the job of `preview_item` is to "encode" preview information in a buffer+position and return it. Afterwards only hope that `vim.ui.select` implementation uses it as intended. Same as with `opts.format_item()`.

> There's currently not a single word that would tell a user who reads the docs that the preview only works if they also install a plugin that overrides the function with one that has that capability.
> Reading a function description and then trying it only to find that it doesn't work as described is an awful experience.

Is this enough?

```diff
diff --git a/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua b/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
index 563db172b8..10042057e3 100644
--- a/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
+++ b/runtime/lua/vim/ui.lua
@@ -12,6 +12,7 @@ local M = {}
 ---
 --- Function to preview an individual item from `items`.
 --- If missing, no special preview is used.
+--- Default `vim.ui.select()` implementation currently doesn't support preview.
 --- Should ensure a buffer with contents (text, highlighting) providing details about an item.
 --- Should return a table with information that `vim.ui.select` implementations may
 --- use to show the preview buffer in the way they see fit:
```

> Which actually raises two more problems/questions:

TL;DR to both answers: the same way as it is currently done for `opts.format_items`. They are almost identical when it comes to the interface: it is documented but up to the `vim.ui.select` to actually use it.

> * Even if core implements it, users could have a plugin that doesn't.

The same as it is currently the case with `opts.format_items`. Plugin *might* not implement this. The solution is to open an issue for plugin to add support or accept that it doesn't work while starting to look for another `vim.ui.select()` implementation.

> * How will configuration of this work? I suspect some users might want preview for use-case a), but no preview for use-case b)? Do we tell plugin authors that they should have config options for it. Or should core have some configuration based on `kind`? First instinct is to delegate that to plugins, but we'll also need to think of the `ui.select` uses within core itself.

Whichever way the `vim.ui.select` implementation (usually picker plugins) allows for configuration. There is no advice for `opts.format_items`, I don't see the reason to start doing so for `opts.preview_items`.

> Imho this shouldn't block 0.12 but have some more time to bake.

It has been baking for quite some time before the issue, for two months here in public, and is long overdue. Any documented way of providing preview will greatly simplify the life of plugin authors, since the tendency of "implement a picker for all currently popular picker plugins" is not great.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: docs(vim.ui): document an interface for `vim.ui.select` preview
